question: Henry Mintzberg identified five fundamental coordination mechanisms that organizations use to integrate the work of different people and units. What are they?
options: Hierarchy, departments, meetings, policies, and technology platforms

Mutual Adjustment (informal communication — two people coordinate by talking to each other; works in very small organizations or for novel, complex tasks); Direct Supervision (a manager coordinates by directing the work of subordinates — the hierarchical coordination mechanism); Standardization of Work Processes (procedures specify exactly how work is done, enabling coordination without communication — assembly line, fast food, McDonald's kitchen operations); Standardization of Outputs (results are specified rather than how to achieve them — sales targets, quality standards, delivery commitments); and Standardization of Skills (rather than specifying the work or the output, the organization ensures all workers have the same training and expertise — doctors in an operating room coordinate by virtue of medical training, not a supervisor directing every cut); as organizations grow and tasks become more complex, they typically shift from mutual adjustment (works at 10 people) to direct supervision (100 people) to standardization (10,000+ people)
Reporting structures, org charts, job descriptions, performance reviews, and KPIs
Formal meetings, written communication, software systems, policies, and physical proximity
explanation: Mintzberg's coordination mechanisms: the taxonomy is useful for diagnosing organizational design problems. When coordination is failing (products ship late, customers complain, handoffs break), the diagnosis often traces to a mismatch between the coordination mechanism and the task complexity. Simple, routine tasks: standardization of work processes works best (no communication overhead). Novel, complex tasks: mutual adjustment required (experts need to communicate directly to figure out the answer). Emergency situations: direct supervision (commander decides quickly, others execute). High-skill professionals: standardization of skills (doctors, lawyers, pilots coordinate through shared professional training). The startup to enterprise transition: startups coordinate primarily through mutual adjustment (everyone talks to everyone); as they scale, they must develop standardization mechanisms without losing the mutual adjustment for novel problems — the classic startup scaling challenge.

question: A large consumer goods company uses a liaison role — a 'Category Manager' who sits between Marketing and Supply Chain — to coordinate the development of new product launches. The Category Manager has no formal authority over either Marketing or Supply Chain but is responsible for coordinating their activities. Why might this coordination mechanism be more effective than using the standard hierarchy?
options: Liaison roles are useful only when organizations have budget for additional headcount

Lateral coordination structures resolve the speed and knowledge limitations of hierarchical coordination: the hierarchical alternative: when Marketing and Supply Chain conflict, the issue escalates to their shared superior (the VP of Marketing or COO); each escalation takes 1-2 weeks; the manager making the decision may lack detailed product knowledge; for frequent, routine coordination needs, hierarchy becomes a bottleneck. The liaison mechanism advantages: (1) Speed: the Category Manager can resolve day-to-day coordination issues in hours rather than weeks of hierarchical escalation; (2) Knowledge: the Category Manager develops deep expertise in both the Marketing perspective (brand strategy, customer insights) and Supply Chain perspective (lead times, capacity constraints); they can translate between the two functions' languages; (3) Relationship: the Category Manager builds trusting relationships with key people in both functions, enabling informal influence that hierarchy can't replicate; (4) Span of integration: one Category Manager can manage the integration for 3-5 product lines simultaneously; the VP hierarchy would need to handle each conflict serially. The limitation: liaison roles work when the coordinator has sufficient credibility and relationship capital; if Marketing or Supply Chain resent the liaison as an intermediary, the mechanism fails; liaison roles also become bottlenecks if overloaded
Liaison roles reduce organizational efficiency by adding unnecessary management layers
Formal authority is always required for effective cross-functional coordination
explanation: Lateral coordination mechanisms: Galbraith's information processing theory of organizational design identifies three types of lateral mechanisms: liaison roles (single person facilitating coordination), cross-functional teams (ongoing coordination through team meetings), and matrix organizations (dual reporting to both function and project). Liaison roles are the lowest-cost option (one additional person vs. a full team meeting) for recurring but manageable coordination needs. P&G's Brand Management system: P&G created the 'brand manager' role in the 1930s — a cross-functional coordinator responsible for integrating the work of R&D, Manufacturing, Marketing, and Sales for a specific product brand. This became the dominant organizational model in consumer goods globally. The brand manager's authority: no formal authority over any function, but deep product knowledge and relationship capital enable effective coordination. The contrast: matrix organizations give the coordinator formal authority over project resources — appropriate when coordination needs are more intensive and sustained. The liaison role is the lighter-weight version appropriate for moderate coordination needs.

question: Google's famous '20% time' policy (engineers could spend 20% of their time on self-directed projects) produced Gmail, Google News, and other innovations. This is a form of coordination mechanism that enables innovation across organizational boundaries. Why might formal coordination mechanisms (scheduled meetings, required approvals) fail to produce the same innovations?
options: Google's 20% time succeeded because it gave engineers additional compensation time

Self-directed exploration enables recombination of knowledge across organizational boundaries that formal coordination can't replicate: the formal coordination limitation: formal mechanisms (scheduled cross-functional meetings, project approvals) are designed to coordinate known work — they can execute on planned innovations. They cannot generate unplanned innovations because: (1) Approval processes filter out ideas that don't fit current strategy (Gmail wasn't in Google's product roadmap); (2) Scheduled meetings happen between people who are already connected by their official work relationships — not between, say, a Gmail engineer and a Maps engineer who might have a serendipitous insight together; (3) Resource allocation requires business case justification — a speculative idea with no revenue projection can't get approved through standard processes; the 20% time mechanism enables: (1) Exploration without justification — engineers pursue interesting ideas without needing to explain the business case first; (2) Cross-boundary recombination — engineers meet and talk to people outside their official team; connections across organizational silos that formal mechanisms can't create; (3) Fast experimentation — small projects can proceed without approval overhead; (4) Bottom-up innovation — ideas from people closest to the technology, not from leadership strategic planning. The limitation acknowledged: only 50%+ of Google's major innovations came from 20% time (many came from acquisitions and official projects); 20% time can create unfocused exploration if not supported by a culture that converts experiments into products
Self-directed time is effective only for producing incremental improvements to existing products
Organizations with formal planning processes never produce breakthrough innovations
explanation: Innovation coordination mechanisms: the research on organizational innovation consistently shows that formal coordination (stage-gate processes, planned innovation programs) is necessary but insufficient for breakthrough innovation. Breakthrough innovations typically come from: cross-boundary knowledge recombination (engineers who know both AI and healthcare, finding an application neither specialist would see), exploration time protected from operational demands, and culture that permits failure (the vast majority of 20% time projects fail — Google's culture accepted this). The 3M model: 3M's '15% time' for scientists predated Google by decades; Post-It Notes (Spencer Silver's accidental discovery of a repositionable adhesive) came from this unstructured exploration. The formal/informal balance: companies that eliminate all exploratory time (every hour accountable to a project code) become highly efficient at executing known innovation but progressively less able to generate genuinely novel ideas. Amazon's 'Working Backwards' mechanism is a formal innovation process that produces incremental improvements; AWS emerged partly from informal experimentation that wasn't a planned strategic initiative. The post-COVID reality: many companies eliminated 20% time during cost-cutting; some (Google itself under pressure) have reduced the policy's practical availability — creating a long-term innovation pipeline question.

question: A multinational company has 15 country subsidiaries that each run their own human resources, finance, marketing, and IT functions. They've been almost fully autonomous ('multi-domestic' strategy). HQ wants to implement a 'shared services center' that consolidates 70% of back-office functions for all subsidiaries into one location (cost reduction target: 30%). Subsidiary GMs resist: they'll lose control, response time will increase, and local customization will be impossible. Both HQ and GMs are partially right. How should the design reconcile these concerns?
options: All functions should always be centralized to maximize efficiency in a global company

Design the shared services center to centralize transaction-scale economies while preserving local judgment for context-sensitive decisions: the shared services center design principles: (1) Segment by decision type: transactions requiring no local judgment (payroll processing, accounts payable, standard HR administration, IT help desk) → centralize fully; these are high-volume, low-variation processes where scale economies are real and local knowledge doesn't add value; (2) Preserve local judgment for context-sensitive decisions: compensation design (local labor market knowledge matters), local government relationships (cultural and linguistic context matters), emergency response (local decision speed matters); these don't belong in a centralized center; (3) Service level agreements (SLAs): the shared services center commits to response time standards (payroll processed by [date]; IT tickets resolved within [hours]) that define the service quality subsidiaries can expect; SLAs make the accountability relationship explicit; (4) Governance: a shared services governance board with subsidiary representation reviews SLA performance and service design — subsidiaries have voice; (5) Phased migration: start with the most standardized, lowest-risk processes (payroll, AP) to build credibility before migrating more complex functions; the 30% cost target: achievable in high-volume transaction processing; may not be achievable if the center is asked to also handle context-sensitive decisions that require local knowledge
Shared services centers always reduce subsidiary responsiveness to the point of failure
All subsidiary customization is always worth the cost, so centralization is never justified
explanation: Shared services center design: this is one of the most common organizational design initiatives in large companies. The economic logic: a company with 15 country subsidiaries each running independent payroll, AP, and IT help desk is replicating the same work 15 times; consolidating these to one center eliminates 80-90% of the duplication. The ERP platform: shared services centers work best when all subsidiaries run the same ERP (SAP, Oracle) — this enables the center to process transactions from any country in a consistent way. When subsidiaries have different systems, integration costs offset some of the savings. The failure mode: shared services centers that also try to centralize high-judgment activities (tax structuring, local compliance interpretation, government relations) fail because the center's analysts lack the local knowledge and relationships that those activities require. The HR example: processing payroll = can be centralized; deciding on the executive compensation structure for the Indian subsidiary = should be done locally with local market knowledge. The hybrid structure — transactions centralized, judgment distributed — captures the cost savings without the responsiveness losses.

question: An airline's operations involve: Flight Operations (pilots, routes, scheduling); Maintenance (aircraft airworthiness); Ground Operations (gates, baggage, boarding); Customer Experience (ticketing, complaints); and Revenue Management (pricing, yield optimization). A single flight delay in Flight Operations creates cascading coordination needs across all other functions simultaneously. What coordination mechanisms handle this in real time?
options: Airlines resolve flight delays through a pre-scheduled committee process

Multi-layer coordination: algorithmic (for routine variation) + human judgment (for novel situations): the complexity: an airline's operation is too complex and time-sensitive for hierarchical escalation or scheduled meetings; a delay affecting 300 passengers and 12 subsequent flights must be resolved in minutes. The coordination architecture: (1) Algorithmic coordination: airline operations software (OCC — Operations Control Center) has pre-programmed decision rules for routine disruptions: weather delay → automatic rebooking of connecting passengers; aircraft maintenance issue → automatic swap of spare aircraft if available; these 'if-then' decision rules handle the 80% of disruptions that follow predictable patterns without human intervention; (2) Operations Control Center (OCC): a centralized room with representatives from all functions who coordinate in real time during non-routine disruptions; the OCC's authority: can reassign aircraft, crews, and gates across the entire network; has visibility into all operational constraints simultaneously; makes decisions that no single function could make alone; (3) Pre-committed protocols: crew scheduling (FAA hours-of-service rules can't be violated regardless of disruption; pre-committed rules determine when a crew 'times out' and must be replaced); maintenance release protocols (aircraft can't fly without maintenance signoff regardless of operational pressure — standardization of skills/authority); (4) Customer communication: Customer Experience notifies affected passengers once the operational decision is made — coordination sequencing ensures communication happens after, not during, the operational resolution
Airlines primarily use passenger vote to decide how to handle flight delays
Airlines don't need coordination mechanisms because flight delays are rare
explanation: Airline Operations Control Center: the OCC (or SOC — System Operations Center) is the coordination hub for major airlines. American Airlines' OCC in Fort Worth processes 900+ flights/day and has 450+ staff working in shifts, monitoring every aspect of operations simultaneously. The multi-function integration: the OCC has representatives who can speak for (and sometimes make decisions for) Maintenance, Flight Operations, Customer Service, Ground Operations, and Revenue Management simultaneously — enabling real-time cross-functional coordination at the speed the airline environment requires. The algorithmic pre-coordination: the most sophisticated airlines (Delta, United) have invested in 'operations research' models that automatically generate rebooking proposals, crew reassignment options, and aircraft routing alternatives during disruptions — presenting human coordinators with pre-computed options rather than asking them to calculate from scratch. The standards of airworthiness: FAA regulations are themselves a coordination mechanism — they create standardized rules that all functions must follow, eliminating the need for negotiation about safety trade-offs. The lesson: the most time-critical cross-functional coordination problems cannot be solved by meetings or hierarchical escalation — they require either algorithmic pre-coordination (decision rules built into software) or standing teams with real-time authority.

question: A startup grows from 12 to 120 to 1,200 employees over 5 years. At each stage, its coordination mechanisms become inadequate. What specific coordination failures are predictable at each growth stage and what mechanisms must be introduced?
options: Coordination mechanisms that work at startup scale work equally well at enterprise scale

Each growth stage requires different coordination mechanisms: 12 employees (startup): primarily mutual adjustment (everyone talks to everyone); works because: everyone knows each other personally, shared context is easy to maintain, no functional silos yet; coordination failure at this stage: almost none — the team is too small for formal mechanisms to be necessary or efficient. 120 employees ('walking dead zone' for startups): mutual adjustment fails (too many relationships to maintain; not everyone knows everyone); direct supervision emerges (functional teams with managers) but is immature; the typical coordination failures: 'left hand doesn't know what right hand is doing' (sales commits to a feature that engineering doesn't know about; multiple teams building similar features without coordination); functional silos form (marketing and product stop naturally communicating); introduces: weekly all-hands (information broadcasting), department heads team meeting (cross-functional coordination at leadership), product roadmap process (formalize what's being built), OKRs (standardization of outputs). 1,200 employees (scaling stage): direct supervision alone fails (too many layers); standardization must carry coordination load; the typical failures: inconsistent processes (the Dallas office does it differently from the NYC office), knowledge hoarding (expertise locked in individuals), misaligned incentives (departments optimize locally); introduces: documented processes (standardization of work), management training (direct supervision quality), internal tools (systems-based coordination), cross-functional governance for major initiatives
All organizations regardless of size should use identical coordination mechanisms
Only the 120-employee stage experiences coordination challenges
explanation: Organizational growth and coordination: this lifecycle pattern is documented across thousands of companies. Larry Greiner's 'Evolution and Revolution' model (1972) describes predictable 'revolutions' (coordination crises) that occur as companies grow: Phase 1: growth through creativity (coordination = mutual adjustment) → crisis of leadership (founders can't personally coordinate everything); Phase 2: growth through direction (coordination = direct supervision) → crisis of autonomy (middle managers need more decision-making independence); Phase 3: growth through delegation (coordination = decentralized authority) → crisis of control (too loose, units drifting); Phase 4: growth through coordination (standardization, systems, committees) → crisis of red tape (formal mechanisms slow everything down); Phase 5: growth through collaboration (teams, matrix, culture). The Stripe example: Stripe (payment infrastructure company) grew from 12 (2011) to 7,000+ (2023); the company has openly discussed its coordination challenges at each stage, including hiring explicit 'organizational design' roles to actively manage coordination mechanism transitions. The management lesson: the coordination mechanism that made you successful at one stage actively inhibits success at the next — companies must be willing to abandon what worked before.

question: An investment bank has separate teams for: Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A) advisory; Sales & Trading; Research; and Wealth Management. Each team serves different clients and has different performance cultures. They face regulatory 'information barriers' (Chinese Walls) that legally restrict sharing of certain material non-public information between Research and Trading. But the business wants to coordinate client relationships across divisions. How do financial services firms design coordination mechanisms that work within regulatory constraints?
options: Chinese Wall requirements make all cross-functional coordination impossible in investment banking

Regulatory compliance creates the coordination design boundary; mechanisms must work within it: the Chinese Wall rationale: if Research analysts share non-public information about company analysis with Sales & Trading, traders could trade ahead of client orders (front-running) — illegal under securities law; the wall protects clients and maintains market integrity. The coordination challenge: a major corporate client (Apple) uses the bank for: M&A advisory (designing acquisitions), Debt Capital Markets (issuing bonds), Equity Research (analyst coverage), and Wealth Management (CFO's personal portfolio) — all separately, which is operationally inefficient and creates poor client experience. The compliant coordination mechanisms: (1) Client relationship team (relationship banker): a senior banker who owns the client relationship and coordinates across divisions; knows who at the bank serves the client; can facilitate warm introductions between divisions; operates outside the Chinese Wall (coordinating client service, not sharing regulated information); (2) Coverage committees: cross-divisional meetings that discuss client strategy (general business objectives, relationship health) without exchanging regulated information; (3) Technology barriers: system access controls enforce information separation (Research team can't access trading books; Trading team can't access non-public M&A information) while allowing access to client relationship data; (4) Compliance oversight: all cross-divisional communication involving client-specific information is monitored by the compliance function
Investment banks should operate as completely separate independent companies to satisfy regulatory requirements
All coordination mechanisms in financial services require explicit regulatory approval
explanation: Investment banking Chinese Walls: the term 'Chinese Wall' (or information barrier) comes from legal requirements under securities law (US: Exchange Act, Regulation FD; UK: Financial Services and Markets Act). Banks that breach information barriers face: SEC enforcement actions (Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs have each settled cases involving information barrier failures); reputational damage; and potentially criminal prosecution. The practical coordination challenge: a client CEO would prefer to work with one banker who coordinates all the bank's services; the regulatory barrier prevents the universal coordination that would be ideal from a client experience perspective. The relationship banker as coordinator: the 'coverage banker' or 'relationship manager' role is specifically designed to coordinate the client experience without sharing regulated information. They can say 'I want to introduce you to our M&A team' without sharing what the M&A team is working on. The technology enforcement: Bloomberg, Reuters, and internal bank systems have physical access controls — a Research analyst literally cannot access trading desk information systems. This technical barrier supplementes the policy barrier. The JPMorgan 'London Whale' failure (2013) involved traders using non-public information in ways that tested or violated information barrier requirements, costing JPMorgan $6B+ in losses and $920M in regulatory fines.
